title: HTTP Requests & Responses
subtitle: Methods, status codes, headers, and the anatomy of every web transaction

sections.title: Anatomy of an HTTP Request
sections.text:
Every time your browser loads a page, it sends an HTTP request. A request has four parts: Method (what to do), URL (where to do it), Headers (metadata), and optionally a Body (data).

The most common methods are GET (retrieve data), POST (create data), PUT (replace data), PATCH (update data), and DELETE (remove data). Each has specific semantics about safety and idempotency.

Headers carry critical metadata: Content-Type tells the server how the body is formatted, Authorization carries credentials, User-Agent identifies the client, and Accept specifies what formats the client understands.

config.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 500 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="10" y="10" width="480" height="180" rx="4" fill="#11131F" stroke="#202338" stroke-width="1"/><text x="20" y="35" fill="#00E5FF" font-size="11" font-family="JetBrains Mono,monospace" font-weight="600">GET /api/users HTTP/1.1</text><text x="20" y="55" fill="#8A88A0" font-size="10" font-family="JetBrains Mono,monospace">Host: example.com</text><text x="20" y="70" fill="#8A88A0" font-size="10" font-family="JetBrains Mono,monospace">Authorization: Bearer <token></text><text x="20" y="85" fill="#8A88A0" font-size="10" font-family="JetBrains Mono,monospace">Accept: application/json</text><text x="20" y="100" fill="#8A88A0" font-size="10" font-family="JetBrains Mono,monospace">User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 ...</text><line x1="20" y1="112" x2="470" y2="112" stroke="#202338" stroke-width="1"/><text x="20" y="130" fill="#5A5878" font-size="10" font-family="JetBrains Mono,monospace">(body — empty for GET)</text><text x="20" y="160" fill="#FF3D8A" font-size="10" font-family="JetBrains Mono,monospace" font-weight="600">Method   URL          Protocol</text><text x="20" y="175" fill="#8B5CF6" font-size="9" font-family="JetBrains Mono,monospace">Headers → metadata about the request</text><text x="20" y="190" fill="#00F593" font-size="9" font-family="JetBrains Mono,monospace">Body → data payload (POST/PUT/PATCH)</text></svg>
config.caption: An HTTP request: method, URL, headers, and optional body

sections.caption: Every HTTP request has four parts: method, URL, headers, and body

sections.title: HTTP Status Codes
sections.text:
Every HTTP response includes a status code — a three-digit number that tells the client what happened. Codes are grouped into five classes:

  • 1xx (Informational) — Request received, continuing. Rarely seen in practice.
  • 2xx (Success) — The request succeeded. 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content.
  • 3xx (Redirection) — Further action needed. 301 Moved Permanently, 302 Found, 304 Not Modified.
  • 4xx (Client Error) — The request was bad. 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 429 Too Many Requests.
  • 5xx (Server Error) — The server failed. 500 Internal Server Error, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Service Unavailable.
sections.caption: HTTP status codes grouped by class — 2xx success, 4xx client error, 5xx server error
sections.callout: Always use the correct status codes. Returning 200 for errors breaks API conventions. 201 for creation, 204 for deletion, 401 for auth, 403 for permissions.

sections.title: The Full Request-Response Cycle
sections.text:
Here's what happens when you press Enter after typing a URL:

  1. Browser checks its local DNS cache — if missing, queries the DNS resolver
  2. TCP three-way handshake establishes a connection to the server's IP on port 443 (HTTPS)
  3. TLS handshake negotiates encryption keys (2 round trips for TLS 1.3)
  4. Browser sends the HTTP request (method, URL, headers, optional body)
  5. Server processes the request — routes it, executes logic, queries databases
  6. Server sends back an HTTP response (status code, headers, body)
  7. Browser parses the response, renders HTML, fetches additional resources

This entire cycle typically completes in 200-500ms. HTTP/2 reduces overhead with multiplexed streams, and HTTP/3 (QUIC) eliminates head-of-line blocking.
sections.caption: The full request-response cycle: 6 steps happening in under 500ms
sections.detail: Modern browsers optimize this cycle aggressively. They maintain connection pools (keep-alive), pre-fetch DNS records via pre-connect hints, and use HTTP/2 multiplexing to send multiple requests over a single connection. TLS 1.3 reduced the handshake to 1 round trip (or 0-RTT for returning visitors). HTTP/3 goes even further by using QUIC over UDP, eliminating TCP head-of-line blocking entirely.
sections.code:
# See the cycle in action:
$ curl -v https://example.com

# Measure timing details:
$ curl -w "@%{time_connect} %{time_starttransfer} %{time_total}\n" -o /dev/null -s https://example.com
